questions.question: In a hard real-time system, what happens if a task misses its deadline?
options: The task is retried next period with no consequence
System failure — correctness requires every deadline to be met
Quality degrades but the system continues
The scheduler raises the task's priority automatically
questions.explanation: Hard real-time systems have deadlines that must never be missed. Missing even one constitutes system failure (e.g., an airbag controller failing to fire within 5 ms). Soft real-time systems tolerate occasional misses with degraded quality.

questions.question: A task set has three periodic tasks: (T=10, C=3), (T=15, C=4), (T=35, C=8). What is the CPU utilization, and what does the Liu-Layland bound tell us?
options: U ≈ 0.795, which exceeds the bound of 0.780, so RM cannot schedule it
U ≈ 0.795, which exceeds the bound of 0.780, but the bound is sufficient not necessary — the task set may still be RM-schedulable
U ≈ 0.550, well below the bound, so guaranteed schedulable
U ≈ 1.200, above 1.0, so definitely unschedulable by any algorithm
questions.explanation: U = 3/10 + 4/15 + 8/35 = 0.3 + 0.2667 + 0.2286 ≈ 0.795. The Liu-Layland bound for n=3 is 3(2^(1/3) − 1) ≈ 0.780. Since U > bound, the sufficient condition fails, but this does NOT prove unschedulability — many task sets above the bound are schedulable under RM. The bound guarantees schedulability below it; above it, you need exact analysis (response-time analysis or simulation).

questions.question: Why does EDF achieve higher utilization than Rate-Monotonic?
options: EDF uses a faster context-switch mechanism
EDF dynamically reassigns priorities based on current deadlines rather than fixed periods
EDF skips deadline checking to reduce overhead
EDF is limited to single-task systems where it performs better
questions.explanation: EDF chooses the task with the nearest absolute deadline at each scheduling point. This dynamic priority assignment can utilize up to 100% of CPU time (U ≤ 1.0), whereas RM's fixed priority assignment is bounded by ln 2 ≈ 69.3% in the worst case (as task count grows).

questions.question: Priority inheritance solves priority inversion by:
options: Preventing low-priority tasks from ever acquiring mutexes
Temporarily raising the mutex holder's priority to match the highest-priority waiter
Giving medium-priority tasks the same priority as high-priority tasks
Using busy-waiting instead of blocking on mutexes
questions.explanation: When a high-priority task blocks on a mutex held by a low-priority task, the low-priority task inherits the high-priority task's priority. This prevents medium-priority tasks from preempting the low-priority task and extending the unbounded blocking period. After releasing the mutex, the low-priority task's priority returns to normal.

questions.question: What distinguishes a real-time operating system (RTOS) from a general-purpose OS?
options: RTOS is always faster than a general-purpose OS
RTOS guarantees that tasks complete within strict timing deadlines
RTOS can only run on embedded hardware
RTOS doesn't support multitasking
questions.explanation: A real-time OS prioritizes determinism over throughput. Hard RTOS guarantees deadlines are always met (missing one is a system failure); soft RTOS meets deadlines most of the time. This requires bounded interrupt latency, priority-based preemptive scheduling, and minimal non-deterministic operations.
